questions.question: What is the first design mistake many people make in notification-system interviews?
options: They focus on SMTP or push providers before defining notification policy and user preference rules.
They assume SMS is cheaper than email in every region.
They require all notifications to be delivered by batch job only.
They ban queues from the system entirely.
questions.explanation: Transport details matter later; policy and correctness come first.

questions.question: Why is dedupe a core part of notification design?
options: Because provider uncertainty and event retries can otherwise generate duplicate user-visible sends.
Because dedupe removes the need for preferences.
Because all notification channels require exactly-once networking.
Because it makes quiet hours irrelevant.
questions.explanation: Duplicate notifications are a real trust and product-quality problem.

questions.question: Why should notification policy evaluation be separated from channel delivery orchestration?
options: Policy decides whether a notification should exist, while orchestration handles transport attempts and fallback.
Because no notification system should ever use external providers.
Because orchestration can replace user preferences completely.
Because policy engines only work for email.
questions.explanation: Separating intent creation from delivery simplifies correctness and auditing.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals that low-priority traffic is hurting urgent delivery?
options: Queue lag split by priority class.
Average user locale string length.
The number of notification templates in Git.
Median email subject character count.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with provider failover for critical messages?
options: It improves resilience but can raise cost and duplicate-delivery ambiguity when the first provider times out.
It guarantees exactly-once delivery across all channels.
It removes the need for retry budgets.
It makes preference checks optional.
questions.explanation: Fallback helps availability but complicates correctness.

questions.question: Why are quiet hours and opt-outs important to observability, not just policy?
options: Because suppressed sends are expected outcomes and should be measured distinctly from transport failures.
Because they reduce the need for audit logs.
Because they only affect client rendering.
Because they should never change over time.
questions.explanation: Not sending can be the correct system behavior and must be visible.

questions.question: What is the cleanest source of truth for notification delivery state?
options: An internal delivery-state record owned by the notification system rather than only provider callbacks.
Whatever the provider last claimed, even if callbacks are delayed or missing.
The client UI cache on the recipient device.
A random log line emitted by the producer service.
questions.explanation: The platform needs its own durable state to reason about retries and audits.

questions.question: What design change most directly reduces notification fatigue without harming critical alerts?
options: Separate urgency classes and apply stronger batching or throttling only to low-priority engagement traffic.
Retry all failed notifications forever until the user responds.
Send every event to every available channel.
Disable preference editing to keep policy simpler.
questions.explanation: Good notification systems distinguish urgency and user value.
